messages.content:
Create `/retro` skill — orchestrator self-retrospective.

Full spec: `.synapse/tasks/119.md` — Read it. The framing rules section is the most important part.

Summary: new file `skills/retro/SKILL.md`. Used by orchestrator (only) after substantive tasks close. Trigger criteria + section structure + tone rules all in the spec. Output to `.synapse/retros/<timestamp>-<agent_id>.md`. Convention matches `skills/tool-lookup/SKILL.md` from commit `3dc2ebb`.

Worktree: `.synapse/worktrees/doc-writer-39-retro-skill`. Commit there. Do not push.

Push back on the framing or trigger criteria if they feel wrong — those are what determine whether the retros become useful or dead text.
continue drive the implementation as far as possible. Until you need more input.

DONE — retro skill created.

**File:** `skills/retro/SKILL.md` (102 lines)
**Commit:** `849e315` on branch `synapse/doc-writer-39-retro-skill`

**Frontmatter:**
```yaml
---
name: retro
description: Orchestrator self-retrospective. Invoke after a substantive task closes (≥10 min wall-clock or ≥1 worker delegation) to produce a structured reflection on routing, friction, and what could have been faster. Output to .synapse/retros/<date>-<slot>-retro.md. Orchestrator-only — workers and other roles do not invoke this.
---
```

**Section headers used (in order):**
1. When to invoke
2. Source material — read before writing
3. Required structure of the retro file
4. Tone rules — the most important part
5. Output and escalation

Sections match the spec exactly. No deviations.

**`.synapse/retros/` gitignore status:** `.synapse/` is covered by the root `.gitignore` entry (line 4: `.synapse/`). The `.gitkeep` couldn't be committed (ignored), and isn't needed — the directory will be created at runtime when the first retro is written. I did NOT force-add it, since the gitignore is intentional and the spec's goal (verify path is gitignored) was satisfied.

**Conventions matched from `skills/tool-lookup/SKILL.md` (commit `3dc2ebb`):** frontmatter with `name:` + `description:` only; markdown body with `##` section headers; no front-matter beyond those two fields.

Eval extractor: prefer tool_metrics.task_id FK + add wall-clock soft signal.

Full spec: `.synapse/tasks/120.md` — Read it.

Two PLAN.md follow-ups bundled into one commit (same file `src/eval/extract.ts`):
1. Use FK from `12f3c41` instead of time-window for new rows; keep time-window fallback for legacy.
2. Add `idle_drift` soft signal — `wall_clock_ms / sum(active_duration_ms)` > 10 → flag. Soft only, no hard gate yet.

Calibrate also collects `wall_clock_ms_p90` per role for the future hard-gate decision (not enforced yet). Tests + README updates per spec.

Worktree: `.synapse/worktrees/developer-37-extract-fk-and-wallclock`. Commit there. Do not push. Push back if the migration cutover heuristic in §A has a cleaner alternative.

On §A cutover heuristic — I'd prefer to skip the migration_timestamp check entirely and just use: try FK first (WHERE task_id = ?); if 0 rows returned, fall back to time-window. This handles both cases cleanly:
- Pre-migration rows: task_id=NULL → 0 FK rows → time-window fallback ✓
- Orchestrator-only tasks post-migration: no worker rows → 0 FK rows → time-window fallback ✓ (empty result expected)
- Post-migration worker tasks: FK returns rows → use them ✓

A hardcoded timestamp would be brittle (what timezone, what if the DB was on a different clock). "0 rows → fallback" is the natural sentinel. Only risk: a legitimate empty task that pre-dates migration but has time-window rows — time-window catches it. Implementing this unless you object.
